coredata.dc:title: Optimization of carbon dot deposition on polycarbonate substrates via non-thermal plasma: Exploring its superhydrophobic properties and pH sensing capabilities
coredata.dc:creator: Selvaraj, Yuvaraj
M․R․, Venkatraman
Govindaraj, Rajesh
Carrasco, Marcos Flores
Natarajan, Muthukumarasamy
Velauthapillai, Dhayalan
Devaraj, Kamalraj
Jaffer, Syed S.
Yoganand, C.P.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of highly photoluminescent nanomaterials that are less toxic, exhibit good compatibility in biological studies, and they are of sizes typically below 10 nm. Plasma processing is a versatile technique for producing nanomaterials suitable for biomedical and sensor applications. Critical parameters such as input voltage, processing time, and gas flow rate play a major role in optimizing the process for depositing CDs onto polycarbonate (PC). In the present work, CDs were successfully deposited on the surface of PC using the DC glow discharge non-thermal plasma technique. Structural and graphitic layer formation of CDs on PC was studied through Raman spectroscopy, amorphous nature of the samples were observed from the XRD results. The uniform distribution of carbons over the PC was witnessed from the FESEM images. The superhydrophobic nature of the prepared films were observed through the contact angles measurements for water and blood respectively. Contact angle results suggest that these plasma carbon deposited films can be used in applications involving blood contact devices. Further, the coated films were subjected to an acid treatment process for the seperation and accumulation of CDs deposited over the PCs and to test its usage in pH sensing and optical applications. The isolated CDs were identified through and TEM microscopic images. The pH-sensitive photoemission property of acid isolated CDs was analyzed using fluorescence spectrophotometer and the observed results from 3D-contour fluorescent spectra revealed that the KMnO4 treated CDs showed interesting behavior that can be used in biological pH sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-thermal plasma
Carbon dots
Superhydrophobic coatings
Photoluminescence
pH Sensors
Superhemophobic coatings

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: 3D-Contour fluorescence spectrum of CDs with the oxidizing agent

SERB sanction order no SR/FTP/PS-146/2012 and SIU ((The Norwegian Centre for International Cooperation in Education) UTFORSK-2016) Project No.10051. The authors highly acknowledge Mr.J. Periyasamy, for his support on HR-TEM analysis.

SR/FTP/PS-146/2012 and SIU (( The Norwegian Centre for International Cooperation in Education ) UTFORSK-2016 ) Project No.10051. The authors highly acknowledge Mr.J. Periyasamy, for his support on HR-TEM analysis. Author G. Rajesh would like to thank the \u2018Conicyt FONDECYT ( Fondo Nacinal de Desarrollo Cient\u00EDfico y Tecnol\u00F3gico )\u2019 Project No. 3190647 for the support.

Authors YS and YCP was making the work and wrote the original draft. MRV- original Draft preparation and discussion. RG, and MFC supported and analysed the XPS part. YCP (DST-SERB) and MN & DV (UTFORSK-2016) was supported financially to this work. KD and SSJ was helped and analysed the Fluorescence part. YCP and SSJ together prepared and reviewed this research work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Raman spectra of the CDs deposited with the input Plasma voltages of 700 V (a), 800 V (b) and 900 V (c).
XRD spectra of the CDs deposited with the input Plasma voltages of 700 V (a), 800 V (b) and 900 V (c).
XPS survey spectra of bare polycarbonate substrate, CDs deposited with the input Plasma voltages of 700 V, 800 V and 900 V.
XPS spectra of C1s and O1s for bare polycarbonate substrate, CDs deposited with the input Plasma voltages of 700 V, 800 V and 900 V.
FESEM images of a) Pure PC and CDs deposited at b) 700 V, c) 800 V and d) 900 V.

Fig. 6 (a, b) Experiment set up for Blood Contact angle measurements for pure and carbon coated PCs, Fig. 6 (c, d) shows water contact angle measurements for pure and carbon coated samples, Fig. 6 (e, f) shows blood contact angle measurements for pure and carbon coated samples.
HR-TEM images of CDs at 900V: (a), (b) and (c) shows the agglomerations of particles at pH-1 and(d), (e), (f), shows the uniformly distributed CDs at pH-11. (Inset (c & f) SEAD pattern).
3D-contour fluorescence spectrum of CDs in different pH conditions:(a) pH-4, (b) pH-7 and (c) pH-9.
Schematic representation of CDs surface oxidation phenomena with KMnO4 at pH-4.
3D-contour fluorescence spectrum of CDs at pH-4 with different concentration of KMnO4 solution at 25 °C: a) 1 μM, b) 5 μM, c)15 μM and d) 20 μM.
Comparative band positions and hybridizations of CDs for various input Plasma Voltages.
XPS analysis Peak positions for the pure and carbon deposited PCs.

head.ce:title: Optimization of carbon dot deposition on polycarbonate substrates via non-thermal plasma: Exploring its superhydrophobic properties and pH sensing capabilities

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of highly photoluminescent nanomaterials that are less toxic, exhibit good compatibility in biological studies, and they are of sizes typically below 10 nm. Plasma processing is a versatile technique for producing nanomaterials suitable for biomedical and sensor applications. Critical parameters such as input voltage, processing time, and gas flow rate play a major role in optimizing the process for depositing CDs onto polycarbonate (PC). In the present work, CDs were successfully deposited on the surface of PC using the DC glow discharge non-thermal plasma technique. Structural and graphitic layer formation of CDs on PC was studied through Raman spectroscopy, amorphous nature of the samples were observed from the XRD results. The uniform distribution of carbons over the PC was witnessed from the FESEM images. The superhydrophobic nature of the prepared films were observed through the contact angles measurements for water and blood respectively. Contact angle results suggest that these plasma carbon deposited films can be used in applications involving blood contact devices. Further, the coated films were subjected to an acid treatment process for the seperation and accumulation of CDs deposited over the PCs and to test its usage in pH sensing and optical applications. The isolated CDs were identified through and TEM microscopic images. The pH-sensitive photoemission property of acid isolated CDs was analyzed using fluorescence spectrophotometer and the observed results from 3D-contour fluorescent spectra revealed that the KMnO4 treated CDs showed interesting behavior that can be used in biological pH sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: This is to state that the data are available from the corresponding authors (CPY). The research data associated with this article are included in the supplementary material of this article.

ce:section.ce:para: The modern scientific world has always shown keen interest in discovering methods for the incorporation of nanomaterials like quantum dots exclusively on polymeric materials like polycarbonate, polypropylene and polyethylene terephthalate for nano-bio applications [1]. These polymer surfaces incorporated with nanomaterials are found to exhibit favourable mechanical and optical properties, bioactivity, wear resistance, and wettability [2,3]. Incorporation or modifications on to the polymer surfaces are carriedout through different treatment methods such as chemical vapor deposition (CVD), gamma irradiation and the reinforcement process by melt mixing techniques [4–6]. Polycarbonate (PC) is one of the members of polymer family which has good mechanical properties and good thermal stability (melting point is 115 °C) compared to other polymers such as polyethylene terephthalate(PET) and polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) [7]. PC can act like a material that can be considered as a replacement for glass due to its transparent property and refractive index of about 1.58 (∼500 nm).

One of the effective method for enhancing the surface and biological properties of polymeric surface layers is the plasma immersion technique, where a low temperature (cold plasma) DC glow discharge plasma is initiated and sustained by using DC, RF, or microwave (MW) power and is transferred to a low-pressure gas environment. In such plasma, certain electrons are free and not bound to molecules or atoms of the plasma forming gas [8]. The accelerated electrons (energetic electrons) induce ionization, excitation, and molecular fragmentation processes leading to a mixture of active species favouring interactions between active plasma species and the selected material surface. It is an advantageous method in material processing compared to the other processes as this method is capable of producing enhanced chemical activity, an intense flux of charged particles and other exotic properties which makes it a potential technique for surface treatment of materials required for specific applications [9,10].

Cheng Wang et al. have synthesized carbon nanoparticles and graphene nanoflakes through a non-thermal plasma process which offers efficient, scalable production of high-quality carbon materials. Methane was used as reactive gas and different gases such as Argon (Ar), Hydrogen (H2), Nitrogen (N) and Helium (He) were used as a buffer. Helium and hydrogen produced highly crystalline graphene nanoflakes (GNFs), while argon yielded mixed products. Nitrogen generated nitrogen-doped carbon with disordered graphitic layers and Carbon dots (CDs) [11]. Tharchanaa et al. demonstrated that non-thermal oxygen plasma pre-treatment improved the antibacterial activity of the cotton fabric's against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, highlighting plasma treatment as an eco-friendly alternative for textile functionalization [12].Thomas Poche et al. reported the fabrication of hydrogenated amorphous carbon (a-C) thin films using plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD) of a cyclohexane precursor. The prepared films was reported to be optically transparent, smooth, and hydrophobic with optical bandgaps ranging from 3.08 to 3.69 eV and the contact angle values were reported to be between 95 and 100° [13]. Surface treatments on thin film surface through room temperature atmospheric pressure plasma treatment technique was reported to be effective in boosting the photoelectrochemical response where a four-fold enhancement in the photocurrent was reported and this has also been attributed to the formation of more intrinsic defects and the reduction in the bandgap respectively [14]. Hamid et al. have demonstrated the use of non-thermal argon plasma to enhance LaMnO3/CeO2 nanocatalysts for NO and CO reduction. Surface treated catalyst was found to possess higher surface area and NO and CO conversion, respectively [15]. Silambarasan et al. have investigated the effects of DC-glow plasma treatment on the perovskite-type La₀.₆Ca₀.₄MnO₃ nanocomposite electrode for supercapacitor applications [16]. Kumaresan et al. synthesized titanium nitride and surface modified titanium nitride nanoparticles from titanium scraps using non-thermal admospheric plasma for supercapacitor applications. Their findings revealed that the surface-modified electrodes exhibited a significantly higher specific capacitance compared to the untreated electrodes [17].

Carbon is naturally black and is insoluble in water, carbon-based quantum dots have low toxicity, high biocompatibility, good mechanical property and is used in automobiles and as sensors in drug delivery system in the medical field [18,19]. In the present study, CDs were deposited over the PC sheets by a low-pressure non-thermal plasma immersion technique using acetylene gas as precursor. During plasma processing, uncoated PCs was kept into a vacuum chamber and the plasma processing was carried out by varying the parameters like input plasma voltage, operating pressure and gas flow rate (for controlling the deposition rate). Here CDs are produced due to ionization of acetylene gas in plasma environment and it subsequently gets deposited on the PC substrate. Surface property of the material such as its super hydrophobicity and super hemophobicity were stuided for the prepared samples.

Further for the pH sensing studies, CDs deposited on PC substrate were isolated through the H2SO4 acid treatment process. Bases and acids have an abundant role in pH sensing of materials due to its property of forming surface double bonds which can be confirmed by introducing KMnO4 of different concentrations, where it reduces the emissive properties of CDs [20]. A portion of the present investigation is examination of the pH tunability effect of CDs through 3D Contour emissive fluorescence spectra, where the change in emission spectra observed due to change in different pH conditions at different oxidation states [21]. The fluorescence spectrum, which exhibits emission within a specific energy band, can be correlated with the concentration of molecules, particularly in pH-sensitive systems where the ratio of H + and OH− ions influences molecular interactions and the efficiency of functional groups used for pH monitoring [22–24].

The information regarding the plasma chamber have been provided in our previous work [25]. It features a vacuum chamber with dimensions of 50 cm × 50 cm × 50 cm and a sample stage (10 × 10 cm²) positioned centrally between two copper electrodes (10 cm diameter, 0.2 cm thickness). The electrode spacing is adjustable, and for this experiment, the distance between the sample stage and electrodes was maintained at 10 cm. The chamber includes water cooling provisions for both the electrodes and the outer chamber via copper tubing. It has five ports for vacuum control, gas flow, and sample handling, with two viewing windows for monitoring. The vacuum system consists of a 1600 l/s turbo molecular pump and a rotary vane pump, with pressure monitoring via a Penning/Pirani meter. For gas control, two Mass Flow Controllers (MFCs) regulate the acetylene (C₂H₂) input at 60 sccm, maintaining a working pressure of ∼3 × 10⁻³ mbar. The Carbon dots (CDs) were deposited on the transparent PC sheet substrates with smooth surfaces and of 1 mm thickness (purchased from Long Last polymers (PC 99 %), Coimbatore, India). The PC sheet was cut into 3 × 3 cm in size, it is sonicated in deionized water for up to 10 min and it is dried for 30 min at 50 ο C in a oven, finally the surface of the films were flushed with N2 gas and it was loaded into the plasma reactor chamber with the base pressure at ∼5 × 10−5 mbar. As the next step, the acetylene (C2H2) gas was introduced into the plasma chamber (see supplementary info file figure S1 and table S1) with a flow rate of 60 sccm and was controlled by a Mass Flow Controller (MFC) with an average working pressure of ∼3 × 10−3 mbar. The CDs gets produced due to the ionization of acetylene into carbon in plasma state where it gets deposited on the PC substrate. Deposition rate was varied by maintaining different plasma voltages [26] i.e. 700, 800 and 900 V with a constant processing time of 30 min. Each plasma deposition at 700 V, 800 V, and 900 V was repeated three times to confirm consistency in carbon dot formation on the PC substrates. After the plasma processing, the transparent PC sheet was found to be turned dark brown which was a physical indication of carbon intrusion into the surface PC layer.

The Carbon dots (CDs) deposited PC samples were analyzed using X-Ray diffractometer (Bruker), Raman spectroscopy (EZ-Raman), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (PHI Instruments) and FESEM (Carl Zeiss-Zigma) analysis. The superhydrophobic nature of the sample was studied using OCA 20 video-based contact angle measurement unit (Data Physics, GmbH, Germany).

The CDs were isolated through the acid treatment method in which the carbon coated samples prepared after the plasma process (900 V) was treated with 50 wt% concentrated Sulphuric acid (H2SO4). Further, the acidic pH samples was treated with KMnO4 and the changes were examined through 3D-contour fluorescence spectroscopy (Agilent Technologies) and HR-TEM (FEI Technologies).

Structural and graphitic layer formation on Carbon dots (CDs) deposited PC was studied through Raman spectroscopy and the spectra is shown in Fig. 1 . The peaks observed at 1355 and 1580 cm−1 correspond to Diamond ‘D’ band and Graphitic ‘G’ bands of CDs respectively. D and G bands are due to sp3 and sp2 hybridization of carbon molecular stretching modes which is given in Table-1 [27,28]. From the Fig. 1 it can be observed that G band is stronger in intensity than D band and these peak positions indicate the amorphous nature of deposited carbon on PC sheet [29,30].The intensity ratio (ID/IG) of these peaks was used to estimate the disorder degree of the carbon materials [31]. The ID/IG ratios of the samples were found to increase with increase in the plasma processing voltage indicating the enhancement of graphitization in the CDs. The Raman intensity was also found to increase with the corresponding input plasma voltages and it is found to be maximum for the samples deposited at 900 V [32]. The untreated sample (pure PC raman spectra is not shown in the Fig) is largely different and there are no graphite or amorphous carbon peaks were found from the spectrum on the PC surface [33].

The plasma processed samples were studied using X-Ray Diffractometer, which is shown in Fig. 2 . The XRD pattern reveals that for the different voltage parameters no significant changes in peaks were observed which shows that the deposited CDs are amorphous which could be acribed to the pyrolytic carbon [34,35]. The peak at the 2θ value of 16.8° was found to be the imperfection of amorphous carbon due to the formation of pyrolytic carbon on the PC substrate [34]. The small hump position at 44.7° corresponds to the diamond phase of carbon. Pyrolytic carbon is a type of isotropic turbostratic arrangement of carbon with isotropic properties (chain-like structure in all directions of crystalline arrangements) [34].

XPS is a useful tool to assess the in-depth profile of chemical composition and its hybridization components. In survey scan spectra (Fig. 3 ), the peaks at 284.93 eV and 533.90 eV are attributed to the presence of carbon and oxygen elements respectively for uncoated PC. Even though the plasma processing of the samples was performed in the vacuum atmosphere under higher input voltage, the presence of oxygen peak (533.71 eV) were noticed. These could be attributed to the elemental composition of PC substrate.

FESEM images of as prepared and acetylene plasma-treated PC films were studied to understand the structure of CDs deposited on PC surface. Plain untreated surface was observed for the samples before plasma processing (Fig. 5a). Fig.5b shows the 700 V processed samples, where agglomeration of the particles on PC can be observed. With an increase of plasma processing voltage (i.e., 700, 800 and 900 V) the surface is found to exhibit clusters of carbon growth on the PC polymer surface as shown in Fig. 5c. Fig. 5d reveals that due to the higher voltage the charged carbon species got more accelerated at 900 V where the surface is found to be dense and dark. EDS spectrum of the samples are shown in Figure S2.

The effect of superhydrophobic activity is called as ‘lotus effect’ is well understood [39]. Comparing the superhydrophobic nature of the film indicates low friction and the similar contact angles for the blood samples are known as the superhemophobic surfaces [40]. Based on the characterization results, the samples processed at 900 V were selected for further analysis, as they exhibited the highest surface coverage and optimal properties. These samples were used for hydrophobicity and superhemophobicity studies. Surface contact behaviour of unprocessed and plasma processed PCs were examined by using contact angle measurement and the experiment setup is shown in Fig. 6 a and b. With the incorporation of Carbon dots (CDs) over the PC surface the hydrophobic and hemophobic nature of the samples improved which is be due to the decrease in the surface energy this could be acribed to the effect of plasma processing as shown in the Fig. 6. The pure and plasma processed samples have the water contact angle of 91.8° and 179.9° respectively (Fig. 6c and d). The blood samples were collected from the healthy volunteer and the experiment was done immediately after the blood collection. The blood contact angles were performed with the unprocessed and the plasma processed samples and the contact angles were found to be 79.7° to 168.9° as shown in the Fig. 6e and f. The superhydrophobic and superhemophobic properties demonstrated through contact angle measurements highlights the potential applications in medical devices, where blood-repellent surfaces are crucial to prevent clotting and contamination.

For the HR-TEM studies, Carbon dots (CDs) were isolated from the carbon coated samples prepared at 900 V through the acid treatment method (50wt% concentrated Sulphuric acid (H2SO4)). After acid extraction of CDs from the carbon coated PCs, the solution was washed with double distilled (DD) water many times until the sulphuric acid got removed. The remaining solution was prepared at differrent pH levels from acidic (pH-1) to alkali-medium (pH-11) by using sodium hydroxide (NaOH). Fig. 7 (a, b, c) shows HRTEM images of the extracted CDs at strong acid conditions (pH-1). CDs observed at higher magnifications (Fig. 7c) revealed that they were formed due to the agglomeration of small particles as evident at lower magnifications (Fig. 7a & b). The inset image (Fig. 7c) shows the diffracted image (SAED), the image infers the amorphous nature of the prepared material. Figs. 7d, e & f corresponding to the alkali condition of pH-11 shows clearly the CDs are present and were uniformly distributed with the average size of ∼8 nm [41]. SAED pattern of the samples preapred at pH-11 is shown as an inset, the image infers the amorphous nature of the prepared material. Energy-dispersive X-ray analysis was taken CDs extracted at pH-1 and pH-11(see supplementary info file figure S3). Few peaks represent the presence of Cu which is due to composition of the grid.

The optical characteristics of extracted CDs in aqueous solution were studied by 3D-contour Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. After the acid extraction process, the filter solutions were prepared for various pH conditions from 1 to 11 the pH which were adjusted by using Sulphuric acid and sodium hydroxide solutions. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with various excitation and emission wavelength values to generate 3D-contour fluorescence. The fluorescence spectrum in Fig. 8 a shows a blue shift as the excitation wavelength changes from 280 nm (emission at 360 nm) to 230 nm (emission at 300 nm). This shift may be attributed to the highly acidic condition of the medium (pH 4), which influences the tunable fluorescence behavior in response to changes in pH. Moreover, the other two contour images of Figs. 8b & c revealed the same value of the excitation wavelength of 260 nm with the corresponding emission wavelength maximum of 350nm. The 3D-contour fluorescence spectrum showed no significant changes at pH 7 and pH 9, likely due to the high concentration of OH⁻ ions (see Supplementary Figures S5 and S6). However, at pH 4, the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots (CDs) was notably reduced, which can be attributed to the increased presence of H⁺ ions. Additionally, the photoluminescence intensity exhibited continuous energy loss above the Fermi level [28]. In neutral and alkaline conditions, CDs were highly stable with the highest emission spectra as shown in the contour spectrum (Fig. 8b & c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3D-Contour fluorescence spectrum of CDs with the oxidizing agent
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 presents a schematic representation of the surface oxidation phenomena of carbon dots (CDs) using KMnO₄ at pH 4. The CDs with different pH values of 4,7 and 9 was oxidized with different concentration (1μM, 5μM, 15μM and 20 μM) of KMnO4 as an oxidizing agent. Fig. 10 (a) shows the 3D-contour fluorescence spectrum for 1 μM KMnO₄, where the excitation and emission wavelength maxima are observed at approximately 230 nm and 305 nm, respectively. As the KMnO₄ concentration increased from 5 μM to 20 μM, the emission wavelength maximum shifted from 305 nm to 390 nm, while the excitation wavelength maximum remained unchanged (Fig. 10 (b, c & d)). This shift in emission is attributed to the surface oxidation of CDs, which also resulted in a reduction of the bandgap with increasing KMnO₄ concentration [42,43]. Interestingly, this peak shift was only observed at pH 4. At neutral (pH 7) and basic (pH 9) conditions, no peak shift was observed, even at higher KMnO₄ concentrations and elevated temperatures near 45 °C. The corresponding 3D-contour spectra for pH 7 and pH 9 at different KMnO₄ concentrations are provided in the supplementary information (Figures S5 and S6).

In this work, optimized plasma deposition on PC substrates demonstrated surface and optical properties which paves way for scaling this approach to other polymeric materials for applications in sensors, flexible electronics, and biomedical coatings. The findings further enhances our understanding of how plasma processing can be tailored for specific material properties. While this study demonstrates successful deposition of carbon dots (CDs) with promising pH-tunability, superhydrophobicity, and hemophobicity, there are a few areas that can be explored further. The amorphous nature of the CDs, as confirmed by Raman and XRD, may limit their use in applications requiring high crystallinity. Additionally, optimizing the plasma deposition process for larger substrates and studying the long-term environmental stability and biocompatibility of the CDs will enhance their potential for real-world applications.

CDs with an average size of ∼8 nm were successfully deposited on the transparent PC surface using the plasma immersion method. Structural characterization through Raman spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction confirmed the presence of amorphous carbon, with indications of phase changes at higher plasma voltages. FESEM analysis revealed increased aggregation of CDs on the PC surface as input plasma voltage increased. HR-TEM analysis of acid-extracted CDs demonstrated a uniform distribution of particles, likely due to the high electric field during ionization, which restricted crystalline phase formation and maintained quantum-sized particles. XPS analysis identified both sp² and sp³ carbon hybridizations, further confirming the chemical state of the CDs. Contact angle measurements highlighted the superhydrophobic and superhemophobic properties of the carbon-coated surfaces, supporting their potential use in blood-contact applications. The fluorescence properties of the CDs were investigated under different pH conditions (pH 4, 7, and 9) and in the presence of KMnO₄ as an oxidizing agent. A notable shift in fluorescence from the blue to the red region (∼100 nm) was observed at pH 4 with increasing KMnO₄ concentration, demonstrating the CDs' sensitivity to oxidation and pH changes. These findings highlight the promising multifunctionality of the CDs for potential applications in pH sensors, biomedical coatings, and surface engineering.

Yuvaraj Selvaraj: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Venkatraman M․R․: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Rajesh Govindaraj: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Marcos Flores Carrasco: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Muthukumarasamy Natarajan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Dhayalan Velauthapillai: Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Kamalraj Devaraj: Software, Investigation, Data curation. Syed S. Jaffer: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Data curation. C.P. Yoganand: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology - Science and Engineering Research Board (DST-SERB), Government of India. SERB sanction order no SR/FTP/PS-146/2012 and SIU ((The Norwegian Centre for International Cooperation in Education) UTFORSK-2016) Project No.10051. The authors highly acknowledge Mr.J. Periyasamy, for his support on HR-TEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2025.105759.

Surf. Coat. Techn.

Polym. Plast. Technol. Eng.
